bestPracticeEntry.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary button.
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. A single primary button directs the user to the most important action.

bestPracticeEntry.guidance: Place the primary button on the right side of a button group in left-to-right layouts. Place it on the left in right-to-left layouts.
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: Users scan in the direction of the layout's reading order. Placing the primary action at the natural endpoint aligns with the completion point of reading. This convention is also established by operating system dialog patterns.

bestPracticeEntry.guidance: Do not use a Button when the action navigates the user to a different page or URL. Use a Link component instead.
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: Buttons and links have different semantic roles. Buttons trigger actions (submit, open, close). Links navigate. Screen reader users rely on element role to anticipate behavior. Using a button for navigation creates a mismatch between the announced role and the actual behavior.

bestPracticeEntry.guidance: Use the loading state instead of disabling the button during asynchronous operations.
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: A disabled button gives no feedback that an action is in progress. The loading state communicates that the action was registered and the system is working. It also prevents double-submission.

bestPracticeEntry.guidance: Use a verb or verb phrase that describes the action the button performs. Two words maximum.
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: Action-oriented labels set clear expectations about what will happen on activation. Short labels prevent truncation on narrow viewports and improve scannability in dense interfaces.

presentation.code:
Save
Add item
Send invite
Delete
Export

Click here
OK
Yes
Submit form data
Go

bestPracticeEntry.rationale: Sentence case is easier to read than title case or all caps. It also localizes more predictably across languages where capitalization rules differ.

bestPracticeEntry.guidance: When using an icon-only button (no visible label), provide an aria-label that describes the action.
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: Screen readers announce button content as the accessible name. Without visible text, there is no accessible name. The aria-label provides one.

bestPracticeEntry.guidance: Maintain a minimum tap target of 44x44 CSS pixels for all button sizes.
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: The WCAG 2.5.8 target size criterion requires a minimum 24x24px target, with 44x44px recommended. Touch devices require larger targets to prevent mis-taps. The small button size uses internal padding to meet this requirement even when the visible area is compact.

bestPracticeEntry.guidance: The focus ring must be visible in all color modes (light, dark, high contrast).
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: Keyboard users depend on the focus indicator to track their position. If the focus ring is invisible against the background, navigation becomes impossible.

bestPracticeEntry.guidance: Button label text must meet a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against the button background. The button background must meet a minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against the surrounding surface.
bestPracticeEntry.rationale: Text contrast ensures readability for users with low vision. Non-text contrast ensures the button boundary is perceivable.

items.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary button.
items.rationale: Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. A single primary button directs the user to the most important action.

items.guidance: Place the primary button on the right side of a button group in left-to-right layouts.
items.rationale: Users scan in the direction of the layout's reading order. Placing the primary action at the natural endpoint aligns with the completion point of reading.

items.guidance: Do not use a Button when the action navigates the user to a different page or URL. Use a Link component instead.
items.rationale: Buttons and links have different semantic roles. Screen reader users rely on element role to anticipate behavior.

items.guidance: Use the loading state instead of disabling the button during asynchronous operations.
items.rationale: A disabled button gives no feedback that an action is in progress. The loading state communicates that the action was registered.

items.guidance: Use a verb or verb phrase that describes the action the button performs. Two words maximum.
items.rationale: Action-oriented labels set clear expectations. Short labels prevent truncation on narrow viewports.

items.guidance: When using an icon-only button, provide an aria-label that describes the action.
items.rationale: Screen readers need a text equivalent for every interactive control.

items.guidance: Maintain a minimum tap target of 44x44 CSS pixels for all button sizes.
items.rationale: Touch devices require larger targets to prevent mis-taps. WCAG 2.5.8 requires 24x24px minimum and recommends 44x44px.
